content.problemStatement:
Chef is fighting global warming and so he is planting trees. The amount of carbon dioxide absorbed by a tree is equal to the height of the tree. Chef generates trees in a very weird way, and your task is to calculate the sum of amount of carbon dioxide absorbed over all possible ways of generating trees.

Given parameters NNN and KKK, Chef generates a tree with NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN using the following procedure :


First, generate an array A=[A1,A2,....AN−1]A = [A_1, A_2, .... A_{N - 1}]A=[A1​,A2​,....AN−1​] of size N−1N - 1N−1 satisfying the following constraints :

1≤Ai≤21 \le A_i \le 21≤Ai​≤2
A1=1A_1 = 1A1​=1
The number of Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1 is precisely KKK


Now, for all i=1i = 1i=1 to N−1N - 1N−1:

if Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1, add an edge between nodes numbered iii and i+1i + 1i+1
if Ai=2A_i = 2Ai​=2, add an edge between nodes numbered i−1i - 1i−1 and i+1i + 1i+1.


It can be proven that the end result is a tree.

The height of the tree is defined as the maximum distance of any node from the node 111. Find the sum of heights over all possible generatable trees. Since the answer may be large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first and only line contains 222 integers - NNN and KKK, the 222 parameters of generating trees.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the sum of heights of all generatable trees modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The only valid array AAA is [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and the corresponding tree generated is {(1,2),(1,3)}\{(1, 2), (1, 3)\}{(1,2),(1,3)} which has a height of 111.
Test Case 2 : The only valid array AAA is [1,1][1, 1][1,1] and the corresponding tree generated is {(1,2),(2,3)}\{(1, 2), (2, 3)\}{(1,2),(2,3)} which has a height of 222.
